properties.USER_Descriptio: Cornerstone Community Development Corporation, Inc. (CCDC) is a public charity established as a 501(c)(3) non-profit organization by Cornerstone Baptist Church under the leadership of Rev. Chris L. Simmons. The primary focus of the CCDC is to promote community growth, development, and revitalization of the South Dallas/Fair Park Community. Its mission is to assist residents in the community through the process of breaking out of the cycle of poverty while becoming contributing members of their neighborhood. The CCDC provides various community services and outreach programs. Its aim is to build stronger communities through impactful initiatives and sustainable development. We offer educational opportunities, meals for people experiencing homelessness, housing opportunities, medical services, and other relief and empowerment services designed to target those in the South Dallas/Fair Park Community. We believe in fostering a sense of belonging, promoting economic growth, and providing opportunities that enhance the overall well-being of individuals and families. In addition, we partner with several non-profit organizations to implement programs to address teen pregnancy, foster economic development, promote education, support prison aftercare, and empower at-risk boys. These programs play a vital role in accomplishing our mission at CCDC.

The garden is available to the children reached through programming at The Stewpot and First Presbyterian Church of Dallas.  Did we mention that the garden is wheelchair accessible?

properties.USER_SiteName: Green Haus Community Garden, at Shared Housing Center
properties.USER_Descriptio:
?Utilizing efficient, eco-friendly design, the GREEN HAUS is our newest model for creating efficient, comfortable, and affordable housing options for rebuilding families and broken lives. To be built soon near the Santa Fe Trail in East Dallas, this all-green (LEED certified), 24-unit community will provide a supportive housing environment for single parent, female head-of-household families with dependent children. In addition, housing will be provided for disabled and older adult (50+) single, homeless women. 
The GREEN HAUS will be built on approximately 1 acre of land with four residential buildings and one shared community building for use by the residents in the community. The GREEN HAUS community is designed to have a low carbon footprint and minimize the cost of long-term maintenance: thirty-year architectural shingles, a combination of brick/stucco exterior, rain catchers for irrigation, use of native plants, erosion and sedimentation control, water conservation fixtures, energy conservation fixtures, healthy flooring and finish materials, and star-energy appliances for energy efficiency.
Residents will benefit from several unique amenities: a shared community building which will offer a computer lab and meeting space for educational and recreational purposes, a common playground with a nearby gazebo, an outdoor grill and picnic tables, and a community garden which will provide better nutrition and food awareness. The nearby Santa Fe Hike and Bike Trail will provide residents with plenty of exercise opportunities.

properties.USER_ARC_Address: 3921 Martin Luther King Jr Blvd Dallas TX 75210
properties.USER_Descriptio:
Grozilla is currently producing a wealth of delicious, homegrown produce and is a major first-step CEA platform to learn from, teach from, and experiment with.  Ultimately it contributes to our overall economic impact along with the other components in our system which includes the successful deployment of the MLK Seedling Farm greenhouse (at the MLK Center in South Dallas) and our Hatcher Station Training Farm, both of which are traditional growing platforms.
Grozilla is a 15-ton, 16? x 40? growing machine operating 24/7 via a state of the art LED light fueled, shallow pond hydroponic growing platform. It is a one-of-a-kind unit that is singularly well suited for state-of-the-art future farming.  From seed to harvest takes about 28 days.  And with a staggered growing cycle, our CEA pros and interns harvest hundreds of heads of fresh, healthy greens every week.

properties.USER_SiteName: Jill Stone Community Garden, at Temple Emanu-El
properties.USER_Email: shoremrcle@aol.com ; kaellan.mcinnis@gmail.com ; hello@tedallas.org ; estrauss@tedallas.orgg
properties.USER_Descriptio:
The Jill Stone Community Garden at Temple Emanu-El was founded and became a Master Gardener project in 2012. Its mission is to provide fresh vegetables to the Vickery Meadow Food Pantry, part of the North Texas Food Bank. The garden is 100% organic, and all of the harvest is donated. One of the Master Gardeners serves weekly at the pantry and acts as a liaison between the pantry and the garden. 

This PlantTAGG-enabled garden is in the northernmost stretch of land on the Temple Emanu-El campus and encompasses 13,500 sq feet. There are 41 raised beds, trellises, additional farmers? rows, and a greenhouse. In 2020 the garden contributed just over 2.5 tons of produce and in 2021, donations are expected to exceed 3 tons. In 2023, we donated 8,000 pounds of produce. As a certified Monarch Waystation, the garden has several beds with flowering plants to attract and feed the monarchs and other pollinators.

One Master Gardener team member is employed by the Temple preschool and uses the garden as an outdoor classroom for an early education nutrition and farm-to-table program. A core group of about a dozen certified Master Gardeners enjoy sharing their knowledge with other Master Gardeners, interns, and novice gardeners from the community.

Each volunteer has the opportunity to contribute and to learn about best horticultural practices in greenhouse management, plant propagation, soil amendments, growing vegetables, drip irrigation, water-wise gardening, composting, and planting for pollinators.
properties.USER_Partners: Dallas County Master Gardener Association, North Texas Food Bank

properties.USER_Descriptio:
Joppy Momma's Farm is a community farm & garden located in the historic town of Joppa, just six miles south of downtown Dallas. Joppa was founded in 1872 by Henry Hines; an emancipated slave. It is one of the last Freedman towns in Texas. Although its history is rich, Joppa has endured extreme marginalization. Though nestled between the lush wetlands of the Trinity River and the Great Trinity Forest, Joppa was federally declared a food desert. Joppy Momma?s Farm vows to change that by providing healthy, affordable foods for the community. We aim to empower, educate, and create greater opportunities for health, wellness, and self-sufficiency through sustainable, regenerative agriculture. We are committed to restoring, rebuilding, and regenerating the community one seed at a time!

We are a year-round Farm that offers locals a variety of fresh farm products, produce, and family-friendly events. We specialize in Vegetables, Fresh Herbs, and Honey, but offer customers so much more! We are a nonprofit that works to create jobs, provide resources, and offer healthy food options for underserved communities.

Because the Jubilee Park area is designated by the USDA as a ?food desert?, residents may have little or no access to large grocery stores offering fresh and affordable foods. Jubilee takes a hands-on approach to this struggle with programs that provide free, healthy meals each week for children and seniors. 
Today, Jubilee serves more than 2,500 low-income residents each year with a variety of free programs and services under five key pillars: Education, Opportunity, Housing & Workforce, Health, and Safety. The Jubilee campus includes the Walt Humann-T. Boone Pickens Community Center, a Resource Center housing a police substation, two Head Start facilities, a senior housing complex, food pantry, community garden, and a three-acre park.

Juliette Fowler Communities is a vibrant intergenerational community in the Lakewood area of east Dallas that provides residential services and health care for more than 700 residents annually. Fowler serves hundreds of young-at-heart seniors, foster families, and young women as they age out of foster care or are abandoned.
Fowler?s community encompasses 20+ acres of park-like settings with hundreds of mature trees, garden beds, and container gardens. We adapt to our seniors? needs to make gardening easy. The Fowler gardens and amenities are used by and benefit residents, staff, chefs, and visitors. The Fowler Dallas County Master Gardener Association (DCMGA) gardening program celebrates its 20th year in 2023 and we are thrilled to have an incredibly talented, organized, and fun group of DCMGs in a long-standing relationship with Fowler residents and the garden program.
Weekly Friday morning classes and garden activities vary depending on the season and the Fowler neighborhood in which we are working. During the heat of the summer and cold of the winter, we adapt to host horticulture crafts, cooking, or container gardening classes. Sandra Zelley, a certified horticultural therapist, leads monthly therapy classes. One challenge is watering this large community garden area due to excessive temperature variations. In the last few years, we have transitioned to native, hardier, and drought resistant plants.
Hundreds of volunteers from corporations, schools, churches, and organizations are educated by DCMGs on best horticultural practices. One example of a corporate volunteer event is our annual Deloitte Impact Day. This event yields on average 1,015 volunteer hours. This includes about 85 Deloitte employees who receive one-on-one horticultural training from 25+ DCMGA volunteers, 20 Fowler residents, and an uncounted number of employees.
Community Amenities:
Sixteen raised (standing height) therapeutic garden beds
Greenhouse for winter seeding, growing orchids, and a comfortable place to work
Sheds with most needed equipment
Certified Monarch Way Station garden
Media room for monthly educational programs
Life enrichment rooms for indoor gardening/horticultural crafts
Test kitchen for cooking and baking
PlantTAGG site with signage for identifying plants

We are an organic community garden, located across the street from Kalachandji?s Restaurant, the premier vegetarian restaurant in the Dallas/Fort Worth area. Our gardeners care for their 4?x10? reserved raised bed plots, and also volunteer time towards caring for this shared space.

In our first season (summer 2010) we grew all the usual veggies such as beets, carrots, sweet potatoes, lettuce, okra, tomatoes, peppers, corn, cantaloupe, squash, beans, etc. ? and also grew other crops such as amaranth, bitter melon, loki squash, and sugarcane. We have been planting and harvesting a beautiful variety of vegetables, fruits, and flowers ever since.

Individual plots for organic gardening and a variety of specialty gardens. Hundreds of dedicated gardeners and community volunteers participate in activities year round, four seasons. Garden types include Plotholders main garden with 87 raised bed plots
6:00 AM ? 8:00 PM, Daily

LIBERTY STREET GARDEN is an innovative partnership between The Meadows Foundation, New Friends New Life, and Bonton Farms that provides survivors of human trafficking a means to earn a living wage as they build a new life.
The garden will provide six members of the New Friends New Life women?s program with a part-time gardening position. The women will work alongside High, a celebrated gardener in her own right, to grow fresh produce, and earn $10 an hour. The apprenticeships are slated to last about 6 to 9 months, with room for promotion.

properties.USER_Descriptio:
Live Oak Community Garden is spread across 1.5-acres and was formed around 2013. In the last 6 years, the ground has been divided into over 57 plot gardens to grow a variety of plants for production. The volunteers and members have experimented with many gardening styles that have resulted in the thriving of the garden.

Live Oak Community Garden functions on donations and voluntary work and also organizes workshops to teach best gardening practices. Irrigation systems, dirt, seeds, mulch, compost, and tools are usually donated by volunteers. At full capacity, a part of the harvest is donated to food banks.

properties.USER_SiteName: Mama Ida's Teaching Garden, at Dallas Farmers Market
properties.USER_Descriptio:
The community garden where people from all over North Texas can learn how to sustainably grow their own fruits and vegetables opened October 2015 and is located directly east of The Shed.ÿThe American Heart Association (AHA), in collaboration with numerous Dallas community partners, broke ground Saturday, Oct. 3, at 10:30 a.m. on its first AHA Teaching Garden to be located at a farmers market. Mama Ida?s Teaching Garden at the Dallas Farmers Market offers custom-designed educational programs for people of all ages.

The space is named for Ida Papert, or ?Mama Ida,? and is a local legend at the Dallas Farmers Market. The tireless work of this long-time supporter ? she started shopping at the DFM in 1953 and formed the Dallas Farmers Market Friends in 1991 ? is a big part of why Dallas gets to enjoy fresh produce today.ÿPapert was more than just a shopper; she was a local matriarch. She could be found at the market every Saturday morning carrying her personalized bag that said ?Ida?s Gotta Have It,? filled to the brim with produce. She delivered preserves to each stall, made from produce bought at the market.ÿShe took this passion for the farmers and enthusiasm for the market and created the Friends of the Farmers Market, a nonprofit advocacy group. In 1993, she joined forces with the American Institute of Wine & Food to start the market cooking classes enjoyed by enthusiastic foodies.
properties.USER_Partners: Dallas County Master Gardeners, Grow Garden Grow, American Heart Association

properties.USER_Descriptio: Since 2019, The Mill City Teaching Farm has been providing nature-based learning for our youth. We are a non-profit 501(c)(3) located in Dallas, Texas. Our goal is to provide educational and recreational opportunities while providing a safe and inviting place for the community. Mill City Teaching Farm is a place for young learners to explore the natural world while fostering a sense of community.

properties.USER_SiteName: Moss Haven Farms, at Moss Haven Elementary School
properties.USER_Descriptio:
A non-profit urban farm located at Moss Haven Elementary. The garden has approximately 1,000 square feet of raised-bed growing space and a slightly larger area for conventional row crops. It is both a prolific, small-farming operation and a STEM-based outdoor classroom.

Moss Haven Farm exists to cultivate a respect for the earth and food-growth process in our students. Our vision is that they will develop a lifelong interest in responsible environmental practices, seek to live a healthy lifestyle, and cultivate a passion for giving back to the community. In addition to enriching academics, our program seeks to spark positive growth in our students? families and our community.

Seeds sown on a simple grassy patch in March 2012 have now grown to 23 raised beds, a farm field, and chicken coop. Moss Haven Farm has been recognized as an American Heart Association Teaching Garden and has been awarded United Way?s Healthy Zone School.
properties.USER_Partners: Moss Haven PTA, American Heart Association

properties.USER_SiteName: Our Garden in Old Lake Highlands, at Lake Highlands Baptist Church
properties.USER_Descriptio:
Our Garden in Old Lake Highlands is a community garden with a bit of a twist. There are no individual plots to tend or dues to pay. This is a community garden in the truest sense of the word. Our Garden aims to bring the Old Lake Highlands community together to grow healthy food and relationships. Volunteers share the work and wealth, but the real reward is the connection and friendships.

We are volunteer-run and community sponsored. We rely on our neighbors' time and donations to continue making this a special place for everyone. We believe access to fresh, seasonal, and organic produce is a right for all. All are welcome to this garden to admire and pick freely. We abide by the ?Honorable Harvest? rules and request you take only what you need.

Texas A&M AgriLife Research and Extension Center at Dallas
properties.USER_Descriptio: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension Service is a statewide educational agency and a member of the Texas A&M University System linked in a unique partnership with the nationwide United States Department of Agriculture?s Cooperative Extension System and the Dallas County Commissioners Court. Extension values and promotes principles of citizen and community involvement, scientifically-based education, lifelong learning, and volunteerism. Texas AgriLife Extension provides access to citizens in all 254 Texas counties and works cooperatively with the 10 universities and 8 state agencies of the Texas A&M University System and other state and local agencies to bring the necessary resources to Dallas County that will address local issues and solve local problems.

properties.USER_SiteName: The Garden of L.I.F.E., at Hamilton Park Pacesetter Magnet
properties.USER_Descriptio:
The purpose of the Garden of L.I.F.E is to introduce students to gardening, conservation, protection of wildlife, recognition of plants, trees and shrubs, and to nourish their inherent love and appreciation of nature. Particularly, we strive to promote good nutrition by planting, tending harvesting and eating organic fruits, vegetables and herbs. By exercising practical gardening skills, we learn the nuts and bolts of growing a garden.
The goal is that they will be future citizens who will be responsible and capable with greater self-expression, dignity, integrity and knowledge of their environment by connecting children to the natural world and create environmental stewards. We also seek to grow character by encouraging leadership, responsibility, and respect when we work in the garden. Children will be able to develop a ?sense of place? as we build a garden that reflects who we are as individuals, as a school and as residents of Dallas. Additionally, we will grow a community, where we will create positive relationships among students, staff, parents and neighbors when we work together.

The Garden at Mockingbird Elementary began over 20 years ago with a simple goal: use a garden to teach children about the life cycle of plants. Today, it has evolved into a 22,000 square-foot garden?a welcome escape to nature in such an urban neighborhood.

This outdoor living classroom is a rare, immersive learning resource in the heart of East Dallas that is utilized by students in kindergarten through 5th grade. It is a key educational tool, helping teach science hands on through experiments and observation of the diverse ecosystem. It also connects children to nature and helps them grow as learners. All students regularly attend garden class.

properties.USER_SiteName: The Hoover Kids Learning Garden, at T. R. Hoover Community Development Corporation
properties.USER_Descriptio:
TR Hoover is a community organization that focuses on providing educational, social and community services to our South Dallas neighborhood. With the help of a team of volunteers, TR Hoover offers a variety of programs to the children and adults in our community. During the academic year, we run an after school program for 40 children and during the summer, we run a 10 week summer camp. These programs not only provide educational enhancement to our kids, we provide a safe, structured environment where they can get homework help, attend field trips and received nutritious meals. We offer ESL classes to our community members as well as weekly programming for seniors in our community. We provide members of the community access to our food pantry and clothing closet once a week. We have a learning garden that is used to not only grow organic fruits and vegetables that are distributed to our client families, but we also use the garden to teach nutrition, resilience and self-sufficiency. The garden's purpose is to not only provide food, but give a sense of community through education, arts, culture and music.

Mission
T.R. Hoover's mission is to strengthen, empower, and provide holistic, collaborative services that impact the South Dallas community through affordable housing, economic, and community development. We advocate and address the issues of poverty, education, economic divestment, blight, infrastructure decline, neighborhood safety, and the rising levels of unemployment.
properties.USER_Management: T. R. Hoover Community Development Corporation
properties.USER_Notes: During the late 1980s, we established ourselves as a grassroots neighborhood association which eventually evolved into the Ideal Neighborhood Association. This association was made up of low-income families who aimed to combat the negative developments occurring in their community. In 1997, a select few from the association came together to create the T. R. Hoover Community Development Corporation (TRHCDC). This organization obtained its 501(c)(3) status and started to tackle various issues such as economic disinvestment, blight, deteriorating infrastructure, neighborhood safety concerns, and the increasing unemployment rates in the South Dallas Community.

properties.USER_SiteName: The Imagine Garden, at White Rock United Methodist Church
properties.USER_Descriptio: The Imagine Garden, a thriving community garden, was built on top of a parking lot at White Rock United Church in East Dallas with over 200 volunteers over three months. For three years, POP served over 250 community members, over 500 children and youth with seed to soul programming, cooking classes, garden art, and farm to table dinners as we connected with hundreds of community members and organizations, with the support of more than 1,500 volunteers!

Over the past five years we have now grown tens of thousands of seedlings and we?re just getting better and more efficient while increasing our output as we learn and grow. We use these plant starts to help us grow delicious produce at out Hatcher Station Farm as well as for distribution and sale to the community for other urban gardens.

Vogel Alcove is the only comprehensive early childhood education program in the city of Dallas whose primary focus is to provide free childcare and case management for children and their families referred by 19 local emergency shelters, domestic violence shelters, housing programs and organizations that serve homeless families.

properties.USER_SiteName: WE over Me Farm, at Paul Quinn College
properties.USER_Descriptio:
The WE Over Me Farm at Paul Quinn seeks to impact that injustice, and has produced more than 30,000 pounds of organically grown produce since its inception. No less than 15% of this produce has been donated to neighborhood charitable organizations, and the remainder is distributed to local community members, the College, and local food business partners. 

As a part of the Paul Quinn Work Program, the Farm is operated and maintained primarily by Paul Quinn student-employees under the guidance of the Farm Manager. Students actively engage in all farm activities, from business planning to marketing, and thus it serves as a model of socially-conscious and environmentally-driven servant leadership and entrepreneurship. In addition to providing fresh, healthy, affordable food options for its surrounding residents, the Farm strives to improve communities throughout the metroplex by providing hands-on educational experiences for youth and adults alike to promote healthy eating, improved food access, and environmental stewardship.

properties.USER_SiteName: West Dallas Multipurpose Center's Community Garden
properties.USER_Descriptio: Garden grow boxes are available for adoption by local community members, organizations, and groups. There is no charge to adopt boxes! WDMC provides soil and amendments, access to water, regular irrigation, seedling plants 4 times a year, and tools you may need to work in the garden. You will prepare, plant, weed and tend to the grow boxes each season. The mature harvest is shared with community gardeners and neighbors.

Worth Street Community Garden is a local Community Garden that brings together community members of all ages from the Old East Dallas area. We?ve been working since 2017 to grow local and healthy produce in an open space that enables residents to be involved in planting, growing, and maintaining a garden. We encourage you to join us and contribute towards making a difference.
properties.USER_Partners: Grace United Methodist Church, Literacy Achieves and The Agape Clinic
